title: Optical Properties of Poly(Para-Phenylenes) under High Pressure
abstract:
phys. stat. sol. (b) 211, 177 (1999)
Subject classification: 78.30.Jw; 78.40.Me; 78.55.Kz; S12
Optical Properties of Poly(Para-Phenylenes)
under High Pressure
S. Guha (a), W. Graupner (b), S. Yang (a), M. Chandrasekhar (a),
H. R. Chandrasekhar (a), and G. Leising (b)
(a) Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Missouri, Columbia, MO 65211,
USA
(b) Institut fuÈr FestkoÈrperphysik, Technische UniversitaÈt Graz, A-8010 Graz, Austria
(Received August 14, 1998)
We present optical studies using photoluminescence, absorption and Raman scattering on samples
from two generic families, the polycrystalline para-phenylene oligomers and the amorphous para-
phenylene polymers, under hydrostatic pressure. These studies have provided insight into the
geometry of the molecules, and the fundamental properties of the ground and excited states of
these materials. Using our measurements under pressure and a simple model we extract atmo-
spheric pressure values for the shift in the configuration coordinate between ground and excited
states and the ratio of force constants describing the potential wells that describe these states.
1. Introduction
Conjugated polymers are characterized by a highly delocalized electronic structure
along the polymer backbone. Undoped conjugated polymers behave as semiconductors
with band gaps of 1.4 to 3 eV. When suitably doped, these materials can display metal-
lic conductivities. The promise of conducting polymers is to combine the chemical, me-
chanical, and processing properties of plastics with the electrical behavior of metals and
semiconductors. Applications of conducting polymers include capacitors, cable shielding,
electrochromic smart-windows, rechargeable batteries and sensors. In recent years con-
siderable interest has been directed towards undoped conjugated polymers as replace-
ments for conventional semiconductors in optical devices. The emissison of light by
polymers in an electric field (electroluminescence) was first demonstrated in poly (p-
phenylene vinylene) (PPV), in 1990 [1]. Significant progress has been made in improv-
ing the efficiency and the range of available emitted colors. Ladder poly (p-phenylene)
(LPPP) has been found to be an attractive candidate for blue light emitting diodes
(LEDs) [2 to 4] and other devices due to its high photoluminescence yield. The oligo-
phenyl para-hexaphenyl (PHP) has also been successfully applied to create the ele-
ments for a red-green-blue color display [5].
Both PHP and LPPP have been used as active materials in polymer light emitting de-
vices (LEDs) and show a high photoluminescence quantum yield. PHP is known to form
monoclinic crystallites of space group P21/a [6], and is characterized by a torsional degree
of freedom between the neighboring phenyl rings. In the crystalline state the molecules
are arranged in layers forming a herringbone type of arrangement as shown in Fig. 1a.
The planes of the two molecules are tilted relative to each other at an angle of 98.17.

to be the best material within the LPPP fa-

can be polymerized with an average conju-
gation length of more than 10 phenyl rings. Both PHP and m-LPPP are known to show
stimulated emission [7, 8]. Highly directional, polarized emission with a linewidth of
about 2 nm is observed from optically excited waveguides [9] of m-LPPP.
Although the photophysical properties of conjugated polymers have been investi-
gated since the discovery of conducting polyacetylene [10], fundamental properties of
the electronic states, such as the nature of the ground and excited states are not fully
understood. The inability to tune intermolecular interactions, and the unavailability un-
til recently of pure materials with a low density of defects has hampered the under-
standing of these materials. With a view toward shedding light on the inter- and intra-
chain interactions we have conducted optical studies, using primarily Raman scattering,
photoluminescence (PL), and absorption spectroscopy to investigate the vibrational and
electronic properties of chosen polymers using PHP (an oligophenyl) and m-LPPP (a
polyphenyl) as models of chain and ladder polymers. Both materials are of considerable
technological importance and high chemical purity.
We use hydrostatic pressure as the parameter that changes interatomic distances and
tunes the structural and electronic properties of polymer chains in a unique manner
without changing the chemical nature of the material. Intermolecular separation is re-
duced, thereby amplifying contributions of the intermolecular interactions to the optical
properties, revealing structural and electronic properties that make polymers more effi-
cient for lasers and other devices. We thus probe interactions affected by the increased
proximity of the chains as well as changes that occur in the geometry of a given mole-
cule. Quantum-chemical calculations have shown that interchain interactions play a ma-

We present optical studies using photoluminescence, absorption and Raman scattering on samples
from two generic families, the polycrystalline para-phenylene oligomers and the amorphous para-
phenylene polymers, under hydrostatic pressure. These studies have provided insight into the
geometry of the molecules, and the fundamental properties of the ground and excited states of
these materials. Using our measurements under pressure and a simple model we extract atmo-
spheric pressure values for the shift in the configuration coordinate between ground and excited
states and the ratio of force constants describing the potential wells that describe these states.
Conjugated polymers are characterized by a highly delocalized electronic structure
along the polymer backbone. Undoped conjugated polymers behave as semiconductors
with band gaps of 1.4 to 3 eV. When suitably doped, these materials can display metal-
lic conductivities. The promise of conducting polymers is to combine the chemical, me-
chanical, and processing properties of plastics with the electrical behavior of metals and
semiconductors. Applications of conducting polymers include capacitors, cable shielding,
electrochromic smart-windows, rechargeable batteries and sensors. In recent years con-
siderable interest has been directed towards undoped conjugated polymers as replace-
ments for conventional semiconductors in optical devices. The emissison of light by
polymers in an electric field (electroluminescence) was first demonstrated in poly (p-
phenylene vinylene) (PPV), in 1990 [1]. Significant progress has been made in improv-
ing the efficiency and the range of available emitted colors. Ladder poly (p-phenylene)
(LPPP) has been found to be an attractive candidate for blue light emitting diodes
(LEDs) [2 to 4] and other devices due to its high photoluminescence yield. The oligo-
phenyl para-hexaphenyl (PHP) has also been successfully applied to create the ele-
ments for a red-green-blue color display [5].
Both PHP and LPPP have been used as active materials in polymer light emitting de-
vices (LEDs) and show a high photoluminescence quantum yield. PHP is known to form
monoclinic crystallites of space group P21/a [6], and is characterized by a torsional degree
of freedom between the neighboring phenyl rings. In the crystalline state the molecules
are arranged in layers forming a herringbone type of arrangement as shown in Fig. 1a.
The planes of the two molecules are tilted relative to each other at an angle of 98.17.

Although the photophysical properties of conjugated polymers have been investi-
gated since the discovery of conducting polyacetylene [10], fundamental properties of
the electronic states, such as the nature of the ground and excited states are not fully
understood. The inability to tune intermolecular interactions, and the unavailability un-
til recently of pure materials with a low density of defects has hampered the under-
standing of these materials. With a view toward shedding light on the inter- and intra-
chain interactions we have conducted optical studies, using primarily Raman scattering,
photoluminescence (PL), and absorption spectroscopy to investigate the vibrational and
electronic properties of chosen polymers using PHP (an oligophenyl) and m-LPPP (a
polyphenyl) as models of chain and ladder polymers. Both materials are of considerable
technological importance and high chemical purity.
We use hydrostatic pressure as the parameter that changes interatomic distances and
tunes the structural and electronic properties of polymer chains in a unique manner
without changing the chemical nature of the material. Intermolecular separation is re-
duced, thereby amplifying contributions of the intermolecular interactions to the optical
properties, revealing structural and electronic properties that make polymers more effi-
cient for lasers and other devices. We thus probe interactions affected by the increased
proximity of the chains as well as changes that occur in the geometry of a given mole-
cule. Quantum-chemical calculations have shown that interchain interactions play a ma-
Fig. 1. Chemical structure of (a) PHP and (b)
jor role in the luminescence properties of conjugated oligophenyls and upon photoexci-
tation can result in the creation of new interchain species.
This paper is organized as follows; Section 2 describes our experimental setup. Sec-
tion 3 consists of the Raman scattering studies of PHP under high pressure. Planariza-
tion effects in PHP and a comparison of the Raman spectra between experiment and
theory are discussed. In Section 4 we present the PL and absorption results of m-LPPP
and PHP under pressure. From the pressure coefficients of the PL and absorption vi-
bronic peaks, we estimate the force constants of the ground and excited state.
The raw materials were provided in powder form by Tokio Chemical Industries, Ltd.
and MPI for Polymerforschung. In order to investigate the different samples under hy-
drostatic pressure, diamond anvil cells (DAC) with preindented stainless steel gaskets
were used. Argon was used as a pressure transmitting fluid in the DAC. Pressure (P)
was measured using the luminescence of a ruby chip located in the pressure chamber.
The pressure range used in this work was between 0 and 70 kbar. A thin film made by
evaporating a dilute solution of m-LPPP in toluene on the bottom diamond surface of
the DAC was used for PL and absorption measurements. The absorbance of the film
was 60% at 1 bar which was our criterion for the thickness of the film. PL spectra
were excited using the 351.1 nm line of an Ar laser or ir filtered emission from a 75 W
xenon lamp. The luminescence radiation was analyzed with a SPEX 0.85 m double
monochromator equipped with a cooled GaAs photomultiplier tube and standard
photon counting electronics. Absorption measurements were carried out using a
SPEX 1702 single stage monochromator with a silicon diode detector and a halogen
lamp as the excitation source. The Raman measurements were carried out using the
514.5 nm line of an Ar laser. The scattered light was detected with a SPEX triple
monochromator equipped with a CCD array detector and a holographic supernotch
3. Raman Spectrum of Para-Hexaphenyl under High Pressure
There have been numerous studies of the crystal structure of the oligophenyls of poly
para-phenylene (PPP), starting with the determination of the biphenyl structure in the
late nineteen twenties. A closer look at X-ray data reveals that PPP and its oligophe-
nyls adopt conformations such that the phenyl rings within each molecule are on the
average coplanar, but undergo librational motion. The repulsion between the ortho hy-
drogens leads to a torsion of neighboring phenyl rings with respect to the single bond
connecting them. In the crystalline environment, however, the intermolecular forces
tend to planarize the molecules. A torsional angle of 27 is predicted in single isolated
PPP chains and 17 in the crystalline environment. We have employed hydrostatic pres-
sure as a parameter to increase the intermolecular interaction and monitored the
changes by Raman scattering, probing the vibrations from individual phenyl rings. We
find that Raman spectra provide valuable insight into the torsional angles between the
The Raman spectrum of the oligophenyls is mainly characterized by four intense
modes of Ag symmetry. It has been observed that the Raman intensity ratio of the
inter-ring C±C stretch mode (1280 cmÿ1) to the C±H in plane bending mode

in the polymer chain [11]. This is also a test for planarity, since simulations show that a
higher number of conjugated phenyl rings result in a lower torsional angle between the
phenyl rings. For biphenyl I1280=I1220 is about 25 and beyond hexaphenyl the ratio of
the intensities is close to unity. Raman scattering of the anions of oligophenyls (going

In order to describe the effect of planarization on the Raman spectrum of oligophe-
nyls theoretically, we have calculated the Raman spectrum of the simpler biphenyl
molecule and compared the I1280=I1220 ratio for the planar and non-planar geometries.
For our calculations we used the Gaussian 94 ab initio program with a restricted Har-
tree-Fock scheme to perform geometry optimizations, force constant calculations, and
dipole moment/polarizability derivatives. While smaller, the biphenyl molecule is suita-
ble since changes in the torsional angle between neighboring phenyl rings are involved.
The planar and the non-planar biphenyl (were the two phenyl rings are twisted by an
angle of 52) molecules were optimized with appropriate symmetry restrictions, i.e., D2h
and D2 symmetries, respectively.
The experimental value for a (non-planar) biphenyl molecule is I1280=I1220  25. Our
calculation yields a smaller value of I1280=I1220  14:5 for non-planar biphenyl, while
I1280=I1220  3:2 for the planar configuration, a decrease in the ratio of a factor of 4.5
upon planarization. The factor by which the ratio of I1280=I1220 decreases between the
non-planar to planar geometries allows us to compare these theoretical results with our
experimental results on PHP, since the initial values for the two materials are different.
Experimentally we find (in PHP) that I1280=I1220  0:84 when non-planar (correspond-

4. Electronic Properties under High Pressure
4.1 PL and absorption of m-LPPP film
Fig. 4 shows the PL and absorption spectra of m-LPPP film at a few selected values of
hydrostatic pressure. The emission and absorption spectra of m-LPPP are governed by
a vibronic progression of the order of 0.2 eV, which is indicative of the coupling of the
C±CC±C stretch vibration of the conjugated backbone. The vibronic peaks result
from the overlap of the wavefunctions of the electronic ground state jwgii and excited
state jweji, where i and j refer to the vibrational energy levels. The emissive transition
that is highest in energy is called the 0±0 transition, and takes place between the zeroth
vibronic level in the excited state to the zeroth vibronic level in the ground state. The
0±1 transition therefore involves the creation of one phonon. A Stokes shift arises be-
tween absorption and PL since absorption is due to chains of many conjugation lengths
while PL emission is from the lowest energy state, corresponding to the energy gap of
the longest conjugated segment. Under pressure, along with a red-shift in the vibronic
energies, a smearing out of the vibronic peaks is also observed, both in PL and absorp-
tion (Fig. 4). This is similar to that reported in poly (p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV) un-
The PL spectra have been fit to Gaussians in order to obtain the individual peak
positions and broadening (FWHM) parameters. Since our m-LPPP sample was a thin
film, the 0±2 transition is not clearly observed in our experiments. Fig. 5a and b show
the peak position and the FWHM of the PL vibronic peaks of m-LPPP film as a func-
Fig. 4. Photoluminescence and absorption spectra of a m-LPPP film at various pressures. These
measurements were taken at 300 K

as a function of pressure (indicated by an arrow in Fig. 4). In addition to the Stokes
shift of the 0±0 peak energies for absorption and PL measurements, the 0±0 energy of
absorption peak versus pressure is sublinear (Fig. 5 and Table 1). Broadening of both
the 0±0 and 0±1 PL peaks is observed under pressure as shown in Fig. 5b.

4.3 A comparison of PHP and m-LPPP
The choice of our materials for this study are models of two generic families: non-pla-
nar PHP with no side chains versus planar m-LPPP with bulky long side chains. Both
materials show a red-shift in their transition energies with increasing pressures. This is
characteristic of a p p* transition; with increasing pressures the dipole moment of the
excited state increases because of increasing charge separation which results in a de-
crease in the transition energies [14]. The red-shift that we observe in our samples is
consistent with a higher moment after excitation. This shift in transition energies has
been observed in other conjugated polymers and is also referred to as the ªgas-to-crys-
talº shift [15, 16]. Table 1 lists the energy positions and the pressure coefficients for
both the materials at 300 K. It is observed that PHP has a higher pressure coefficient
for both the 0±0 and the 0±1 transitions compared to m-LPPP.
The broadening parameter (FWHM) of the 0±0 and 0±1 transition as a function of
pressure increases for m-LPPP but there is no significant broadening in PHP. Broaden-
ing of PL emission indicates increased interchain coupling between two conjugated
backbones. Absorption measurements in m-LPPP also show a broadening of the vibro-
A broadening of the vibronics in the m-LPPP sample with increasing pressure implies
that the compressibility of the ground and excited states are different; we use the analysis
in [14] to obtain the ratio of the force constants of the ground and excited states. As-
suming a single configuration coordinate Q, the force constants of the ground and the
excited states are represented by k and k0, respectively. In pressure-induced thermal
and optical processes, pressure selects the volume of the system as the appropriate con-
figuration coordinate. At atmospheric pressure, the potential well of the ground state is
displaced from the excited state by D and the energy difference between the bottom of
the wells is represented by Eth. Upon application of pressure P the potential energy

The origin of the difference between PHP and m-LPPP is not fully understood at
present. There are several basic differences between the two materials: the PHP is poly-
crystalline, has no intermolecular spacers, has librational modes due to the torsional
angle between neighboring phenyl rings and has a herringbone structure so that neigh-
boring molecules are not coplanar. In contrast, the m-LPPP is amorphous, has large
sidegroups that act as spacers, does not exhibit torsion between neighboring phenyls
due to its coplanar arrangement. The electronic structure of the materials is strongly
dependent on these molecular arrangements, and detailed electronic structure calcula-
tions are currently in progress to elucidate these issues.
In conclusion we have presented Raman scattering, photoluminescence, and absorp-
tion spectra of polycrystalline and amorphous powder samples of phenylene-type
polymers and oligophenyls at hydrostatic pressures up to 70 kbar. The relative inten-
sity of the inter-ring C±C stretch Raman mode at 1280 cmÿ1 to the C±H bend Ra-
man mode at 1220 cmÿ1 provides insight into the torsional angles between the phenyl
rings using pressure as a toool for tuning the intermolecular interaction. Planarization
under pressure is observed via the change in the ratio of intensity of the 1280 to the
1220 cmÿ1 Raman modes I1280=I1220). Experimentally we obtain (I1280=I1220non planar=
I1280=I1220planar  2:8 while the calculations for a biphenyl molecule yield 4.5.
In polycrystalline PHP the shift in the PL peaks is stronger than in the amorphous
m-LPPP sample with increasing pressures. In m-LPPP the 0±1 peak shifts at ÿ2.08 eV/kbar
and in PHP the 0±1 peak has a pressure coefficient of ÿ5.31 eV/kbar. The m-LPPP
sample shows broadening of the vibronic peaks. From the broadening and the energy
positions of the 0±0 peak versus pressure we obtain an average value of the ratio of
the force constant of the ground state to that of the excited state k=k0 to be 1.54 and
the shift in the configuration coordinate of the excited state from the ground state to
